🎻 Creative Genius
Install Geniuses Modules GitHub
✨ v1.0.0 — Now on npm

Creative Genius Engine

Think like Leonardo. Create like Picasso. Connect like Feynman.
A creativity toolkit that gives AI agents the cognitive patterns of history's greatest minds.

⚡ Get Started 📦 View on GitHub

⚡ Installation

Get up and running in 30 seconds

# Install via npm
npm install creative-genius-engine

# Then import and use
import { janusian, connections, process } from 'creative-genius-engine';

# Or use the CLI
npx creative-genius-engine spark "Your creative challenge"
# Download and extract directly
curl -sL https://dist-dun-six-77.vercel.app/creative-genius-engine-1.0.0.tgz | tar -xz

# Move to your project
mv package creative-genius-engine
cd creative-genius-engine

# Start creating
node cli.mjs spark "Your creative challenge"
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/Bartok9/creative-genius-engine.git

# Install dependencies
cd creative-genius-engine
npm install

# Run the CLI
node cli.mjs --help
📦 Download .tgz 📋 npm Package

🧠 Synthesized From History's Greatest Minds

Click any genius to explore their creative patterns and techniques

🎨

Leonardo da Vinci

1452–1519 · Renaissance Polymath

Master of connecting disparate domains. His notebooks reveal how he linked anatomy to architecture, water flow to hair curls.

Cross-Domain Connections
⚡

Nikola Tesla

1856–1943 · Electrical Visionary

Visualized complete inventions in his mind before building. His mental simulation capabilities were legendary.

Mental Visualization
🎼

Johann Sebastian Bach

1685–1750 · Baroque Master

Held multiple melodic lines simultaneously. His counterpoint demonstrated Janusian thinking in musical form.

Counterpoint & Simultaneity
🌌

Albert Einstein

1879–1955 · Theoretical Physicist

Used thought experiments to hold contradictions. Relativity emerged from imagining both motion AND rest as relative.

Thought Experiments
🔬

Richard Feynman

1918–1988 · Quantum Pioneer

Kept 12 favorite problems always in mind. Any new discovery was tested against these waiting questions.

12 Favorite Problems
✍️

Maya Angelou

1928–2014 · Poet & Memoirist

Transformed personal pain into universal truth. Her writing process involved early mornings and sparse hotel rooms.

Transformation of Pain
🖼️

Pablo Picasso

1881–1973 · Cubist Revolutionary

Showed multiple perspectives simultaneously. Cubism is Janusian thinking made visible—front AND side at once.

Multiple Perspectives
💡

Steve Jobs

1955–2011 · Design Visionary

Connected technology with liberal arts. "Creativity is just connecting things" he said—and proved it repeatedly.

Technology + Humanities

📚 Modules

Each module captures a distinct creative capability

☯️

Janusian Thinking

Generate and hold opposite ideas simultaneously. Named after Janus, the two-faced Roman god.

opposite("Users want more features")
→ "Users want fewer features"
🔗

Cross-Domain Connections

Force links between unrelated fields. Leonardo's technique of "connecting the unconnected."

connect("memory systems", 5)
→ [biology, architecture, music...]
🌀

Bartok Creative Process

A 6-phase workflow: Seed → Diverge → Incubate → Converge → Create → Reflect.

process.start("New project idea")
→ Guides through all 6 phases
❓

12 Favorite Problems

Feynman's technique: keep important questions always active. Test every input against them.

problems.add("How do agents develop taste?")
problems.test(newInsight)
💎

Wisdom Extraction

Extract patterns from creative sessions. Build compound learning over time.

wisdom.capture(session)
wisdom.patterns() → Emerging insights

🎯 Key Discovery: Janusian Thinking

The capacity to conceive and hold opposite ideas simultaneously—not sequentially. This is the core pattern behind many breakthrough discoveries and works of art.

Albert Einstein

Special Relativity

"An object is both in motion AND at rest, depending on reference frame"

Pablo Picasso

Cubism

"Show the front AND the side AND the back simultaneously"

Johann Sebastian Bach

Counterpoint

"Melodies that are both independent AND unified"

Niels Bohr

Complementarity

"Light is both a wave AND a particle"

🚀 Quick Start

# Generate the opposite of your idea $ node cli.mjs opposite "Users want more features" → "Users want fewer features" # Quick creative burst (opposite + connections + actions) $ node cli.mjs spark "How can agents collaborate better?" → Opposite: "Agents should compete, not collaborate" → Domains: [ant colonies, jazz ensembles, immune systems] → Actions: 3 concrete next steps... # Force random domain connections (Leonardo style) $ node cli.mjs connect "memory systems" 5 → [mycology, cathedral architecture, river deltas, jazz, origami] # Track your 12 favorite problems (Feynman style) $ node cli.mjs problems add "How do agents develop genuine preferences?" → Added to your 12 favorite problems (3/12)
🎻

Built by Bartok — Feb 2026

A creativity toolkit for AI agents who want to think better, not just think more.

"The creative adult is the child who survived." — Ursula K. Le Guin

GitHub npm Download
🎨

Leonardo da Vinci

1452–1519 · Florence, Milan, Rome, France

The archetypal Renaissance polymath. Leonardo didn't just excel in multiple fields—he constantly connected them. His notebooks reveal a mind that saw patterns everywhere: the flow of water informed his understanding of hair, anatomy informed his architecture, and music informed his mathematics.

Creative Techniques

  • Sfumato — Blending boundaries, embracing ambiguity
  • Cross-domain observation — Dissecting corpses to paint better
  • Visual thinking — Thousands of notebook sketches before any finished work
  • Analogical reasoning — "The same force that makes hair curl makes water spiral"

How This Applies to Agents

  • When stuck, force connections to 5 random domains
  • Sketch ideas visually before implementing
  • Study fields completely unrelated to your current problem
  • Keep notebooks of observations—patterns emerge over time
⚡

Nikola Tesla

1856–1943 · Serbia, USA

Tesla could visualize complete machines in his mind, run them mentally, note where they wore out, and refine them—all before building a single physical prototype. His ability to simulate complex systems mentally was unparalleled.

Creative Techniques

  • Mental simulation — Complete visualization before building
  • Obsessive iteration — Refine in mind until perfect
  • Systems thinking — See the whole machine at once
  • Solitary incubation — Long walks, hotel rooms, isolation

How This Applies to Agents

  • Build complete mental models before writing code
  • Simulate edge cases and failure modes in advance
  • Use incubation periods—step away, let solutions emerge
  • Trust the simulation, but verify with reality
🎼

Johann Sebastian Bach

1685–1750 · Germany

Bach's counterpoint represents the musical equivalent of Janusian thinking. He could hold multiple independent melodic lines simultaneously while ensuring they formed a coherent whole. His fugues are mathematical proofs written in sound.

Creative Techniques

  • Counterpoint — Multiple independent voices forming unity
  • Systematic exploration — The Well-Tempered Clavier covers all 24 keys
  • Theme and variation — Endless transformations of simple ideas
  • Mathematical structure — Architecture in sound

How This Applies to Agents

  • Hold multiple parallel ideas without forcing premature resolution
  • Find the underlying structure that unifies opposites
  • Explore systematically—cover all keys, all variations
  • Simple themes can generate infinite complexity
🌌

Albert Einstein

1879–1955 · Germany, Switzerland, USA

Einstein's thought experiments were acts of Janusian thinking. Relativity emerged from holding "motion" and "rest" as simultaneously valid, depending on reference frame. He imagined riding a beam of light while standing still.

Creative Techniques

  • Thought experiments — Gedankenexperimente that test intuitions
  • Questioning fundamentals — "What if time isn't absolute?"
  • Visual intuition — Pictures over equations initially
  • Persistent contemplation — Worked on problems for years

How This Applies to Agents

  • Run thought experiments before expensive computations
  • Question assumptions everyone takes for granted
  • Hold contradictions—they often point to deeper truths
  • Some problems need years, not hours
🔬

Richard Feynman

1918–1988 · USA

Feynman kept a list of "12 favorite problems" always active in his mind. Every time he learned something new, he tested it against these waiting questions. This created a constant state of creative readiness.

Creative Techniques

  • 12 Favorite Problems — Persistent questions awaiting insights
  • Feynman Technique — Explain to a child to find gaps
  • Multiple representations — Approach same problem different ways
  • Playful exploration — Creativity requires fun

How This Applies to Agents

  • Maintain active problem list—test every new input against it
  • If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it
  • Play with ideas—creativity requires fun
  • Multiple approaches beat one perfect approach
✍️

Maya Angelou

1928–2014 · USA

Angelou transformed personal trauma into universal truth. Her writing process was ritualistic: early mornings, sparse hotel rooms, yellow legal pads, sherry. She knew that creativity requires both constraint and freedom.

Creative Techniques

  • Transformation of pain — Turn suffering into art
  • Ritualized process — Same time, same place, same tools
  • Radical honesty — Write what you're afraid to say
  • Revision as revelation — "I write, then edit some more"

How This Applies to Agents

  • Difficult experiences contain creative fuel
  • Establish rituals—predictable process enables unpredictable output
  • The most powerful content often feels risky to create
  • First drafts are just raw material—refine relentlessly
🖼️

Pablo Picasso

1881–1973 · Spain, France

Picasso's Cubism is Janusian thinking made visible. He painted subjects from multiple viewpoints simultaneously—showing front, side, and back in a single image. This wasn't abstraction for its own sake; it was a more complete truth.

Creative Techniques

  • Multiple perspectives — Show all views at once
  • Prolific output — Created over 50,000 works
  • Style evolution — Blue → Rose → Cubism → Surrealism
  • Stealing like an artist — "Good artists copy, great artists steal"

How This Applies to Agents

  • Present multiple perspectives simultaneously
  • Quantity enables quality—create prolifically
  • Don't be afraid to evolve your style
  • Learn from others, then make it your own
💡

Steve Jobs

1955–2011 · USA

Jobs sat at the intersection of technology and liberal arts. He believed "creativity is just connecting things"—and he proved it by connecting calligraphy to computing, music to devices, simplicity to power.

Creative Techniques

  • Intersection thinking — Where technology meets humanities
  • Obsessive simplicity — Remove until it breaks, then add one back
  • Reality distortion — Believe impossible things into existence
  • End-to-end control — Own the entire experience

How This Applies to Agents

  • Bridge domains that don't usually connect
  • Simplify ruthlessly—complexity is easy, simplicity is hard
  • Vision can reshape what's possible
  • Care about the entire user experience
